title: On truncations of hierarchical equations of motion for finite-dimensional systems
abstract: We study truncations of hierarchical equations of motion (HEOM) for finite-dimensional open quantum systems. We prove that for finite-dimensional approximations constructed with a Schur-complement type of terminator, the spectrum converges to that of the full HEOM as the truncation depth increases. We also prove that this approximation is free of spectral pollution: sufficiently deep truncations do not produce spurious unstable modes, provided the exact HEOM is stable. We illustrate the results for the spin-boson model.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous mathematical foundation for the truncation of the Hierarchical Equations of Motion (HEOM). It proves that a specific truncation method using a Schur-complement terminator prevents 'spectral pollution,' meaning it won't create fake unstable modes. Furthermore, it demonstrates that as the hierarchy depth increases, the calculated spectrum converges to the true spectrum of the infinite hierarchy. This ensures that numerical simulations of open quantum systems remain physically reliable.
main_problem: The paper investigates the mathematical stability and spectral convergence of truncated Hierarchical Equations of Motion (HEOM) for finite-dimensional open quantum systems, specifically addressing the issue of spectral pollution.
main_result: The author proves that using a Schur-complement-type terminator ensures that the spectrum of the truncated Liouvillian converges to the full spectrum and that the approximation is free of spurious unstable modes (spectral pollution) if the exact system is stable.
method: The study employs a Schur-complement-type terminator construction, Gershgorin-type theorems for operator-valued matrices, and resolvent analysis to bound the influence of the discarded hierarchy tail.
model_or_system: The framework applies to finite-dimensional open quantum systems described by the HEOM formalism, with the spin-boson model used as a primary illustrative example.
key_observables: The spectrum of the Liouvillian (eigenvalues, poles, and residues) and the stability/gap properties of the dynamics.
important_parameters: The truncation depth (or decay rate threshold gamma*), coupling strength (alpha), frequency (omega_0), and damping rate (eta).
main_assumptions: The proofs assume finite-dimensional systems, positive real parts for decay rates, and an exhausting sequence of truncations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 plots the eigenvalues of truncated Liouvillians for various truncation depths, visually demonstrating the convergence of eigenvalues with the largest real parts as the depth increases.
paper_structure: The paper establishes resolvent bounds, defines the block decomposition and Schur-complement terminator, proves spectral convergence, and concludes with a proof of stability and numerical illustration.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers using HEOM to simulate non-Markovian dynamics, as it provides a rigorous mathematical guarantee that numerical truncations do not introduce artificial instabilities or unphysical spectral features.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Eigenvalues of truncated Liouvillians LT(γ∗). Here, α = 2, ω0 = 2, and η = 0.5. Poles and residues of coth approximation are given in Tab. 1.
